Hello reader, In today’s newsletter: Artificial intelligence: Meet the UAE’s first virtual family Real estate: 636 luxury villas coming to Dubai Islands Property sales: Dubai real estate sector sees $4.4bn of transactions Diamond theft: Dubai Police thwart jewellery gang heist Digital ID: Saudi Arabia approves non-resident foreigner property rules Discover the latest stories from Arabian Business, in case you missed them…

Hello reader,

In today’s newsletter:

Artificial intelligence: Meet the UAE’s first virtual familyReal estate: 636 luxury villas coming to Dubai IslandsProperty sales: Dubai real estate sector sees $4.4bn of transactionsDiamond theft: Dubai Police thwart jewellery gang heistDigital ID: Saudi Arabia approves non-resident foreigner property rules

Discover the latest stories from Arabian Business, in case you missed them on Monday:

Digital Dubai has revealed the UAE’s first Emirati virtual family,  [https://www.arabianbusiness.com/culture-society/meet-the-uaes-first-virtual-family-latifa-tops-community-vote?utm_medium=email]following an interactive community initiative that attracted nearly 14,000 participants. The campaign invited the public to vote for the name of the first Emirati virtual character, with “Latifa” securing 43 per cent of the votes.

Nakheel has awarded a contract worth $708m to Fibrex Contracting for the construction of the Bay Villas project at Dubai Islands. [https://www.arabianbusiness.com/industries/real-estate/636-luxury-villas-coming-to-dubai-islands-as-nakheel-awards-708m-contract?utm_medium=email] The landmark development will feature 636 luxury units across five distinct property types.

The  Dubai real estate sector recorded $4.4bn of transactions last week,  [https://www.arabianbusiness.com/industries/real-estate/dubai-real-estate-sector-recorded-4-4bn-of-transactions-last-week-including-27m-apartment?utm_medium=email]with sales dominating figures, with $3bn, according to Land Department data. In total there were 4,695 sales recorded between August 11 and August 15.

Dubai Police needed less than a day to arrest a group of thieves that had planned the theft of an extremely rare $25m diamond  [https://www.arabianbusiness.com/culture-society/dubai-police-solve-heist-of-extremely-rare-25mn-diamond-in-less-than-a-day?utm_medium=email]for more than a year. The three suspects hatched a plan which involved tracking the arrival of the 21.25-carat diamond in Dubai before trying to steal it.

Saudi Arabia’s Cabinet has approved the use of a digital identity system to allow non-Saudi, non-resident foreigners to own property in the Kingdom,  [https://www.arabianbusiness.com/industries/real-estate/saudi-arabia-approves-digital-id-use-for-non-resident-foreigners-to-own-property?utm_medium=email]t

Alric suggests to send another letter to Hagenauer regarding the financial dire situation of HAG. He thinks its important for out argumentation not to pay at the moment. Pls see below!

BR Thomas

Von: Blaschka Arndt <A.Blaschka@eh.at>
Gesendet: Mittwoch, 11. Februar 2026 19:35
An: Thomas Leitner | Brisengroup <thomas.leitner@brisengroup.com>
Cc: Ofenheimer Alric <A.Ofenheimer@eh.at>; Götz Niki <N.Goetz@eh.at>
Betreff: AW: [EXTERN] your letter 4/2/2026 [EH-AT.FID2087]

Dear Thomas,

Thank you for forwarding the letter to Hagenauer to us. The deletion of the paragraph on the second page is not ideal – as explained in detail before, it was included for specific legal reasons:

There are good arguments that the right of retention (Zurückbehaltungsrecht) under Section 1052, first sentence, ABGB is not applicable to Brisen yet, because the payment in instalments is viewed as a prepayment which does not result in the fee under the TC contract to be due until the final invoice is issued (as this is argued by Hagenauer).

Therefore we have included another layer of defense in the overall strategy: The defense of uncertainty (Unsicherheiteneinrede) pursuant to Section 1052, second sentence of the Austrian Civil Code (ABGB), which also allows to hold back payments.

This defense of uncertainty seems to be well arguable, as the information available to us gives rise to well-founded and objectively justified concerns about Hagenauer’s ability to perform its remaining contractual obligations fully and properly - considering indications of serious financial distress, inadequate progress on the construction site, and the significant delay in the publication of the 2024 financial statements.

We therefore highly recommend to follow-up the letter with an email as follows:

Dear Christine, dear Martin,

Since autumn we have serious doubts as to whether all outstanding works will or can be performed by Hagenauer.

--- Blaschka Arndt (2026-03-02 14:48) ---
Dear Thomas,

We have asked Dr Kleisinger whether insurance cover is available for the following cases:


  *   TU (Hagenauer) does not complete its services (because it refuses to do so) or is unable to complete them (e.g. due to insolvency)
  *   TU (Hagenauer) fails to meet its warranty obligations (because it refuses to do so) or is unable to meet them (e.g. due to insolvency)
  *   The TU contract is terminated and there are therefore no longer any claims for performance or warranty against Hagenauer

Below is his response for your information, which could be translated as follows:

The insurance policies do not include any performance guarantees or default coverage. Warranty claims are also not covered.
Only consequential damage or accompanying damage caused by rectification work would potentially be covered.

Kind regards
Arndt

title: Qualification as a de facto managing director [EH-AT.FID2087]
raw_content:
Email Thread: Qualification as a de facto managing director [EH-AT.FID2087]
Date: 2026-03-28
Participants: a.ofenheimer@eh.at, dvallen@brisengroup.com, edita.vallen@brisengroup.com, hagbrisen@eh.at
Messages: 1

--- Ofenheimer Alric (2026-03-28 06:02) ---
Dear Edita,
dear Dimitry,

Due to Hagenauer's repeated allegations that you are de facto managing directors (faktische Geschäftsführer) of Brisen Development GmbH we would like to briefly explain what de facto managing directors are and which risks such qualification would entail.

Let us, however, note upfront that we do not see any strong argument that this applies to you. In particular, as Dimitry has evidently signed the total contractor agreement on the basis of a PoA issued by Brisen Dev and also has always acted on the basis of this PoA. Further, Thomas and Conrad, as managing directors of Brisen Dev, have signed the amendment to the total contractor agreement in 2023.

A “de facto managing director” ("faktischer Geschäftsführer") is generally understood to be a person who manages the company without having been effectively appointed as a managing director; for example, this could be the case when the actually appointed managing directors, acting as figureheads, do not exercise their corporate functions and instead another person (e.g., a majority shareholder) actually manages the company, typically in a manner recognizable to the public that he/she acts as a managing director (Rauter, Managing Director Liability and Its “Dependence” on Governance Systems, JAP 2025/2026/12).

Whether a de facto management exists is a matter of the individual case. Case law has produced certain principles. In particular a de facto management does not exist if a shareholder or third party merely exercises its own legitimate interests in a permissible manner, such as rights to information, co-management rights, or contractually granted control and participation rights (Kreuzer, De Facto Management, CFOaktuell 2025, 171).

In the case of Brisen Dev we do not see any indication that the appointed managing directors are figureheads. They truly represent the company, are actively involved in the day-to-day operations and they act as managing directors in their dealings with the public. The provision in the total contractor agreement, according to which only you are authorized to make binding statements on behalf of Brisen, is in our view merely a contractual control and participation right regarding the total contractor agreement which in no way reflects the management structure of Brisen. We also do not see that the wording rules out that the managing directors of Brisen Dev may make binding statements or enter into agreements with Hagenauer. This is also supported by the fact that in 2023 Conrad and Thomas signed the amendment to the total contractor agreement.

De facto managing directors (like appointed managing directors) are generally exposed to the following risks (Lobnik, The Liability of De

Dear all,

Attached please find our comments as markup for your review.

Should we maybe also add the question, whether it is true that Brisen is not paying the subcontractors (or something similar)? Right now it is not addressed directly but rather mentioned in the context of the outstanding invoices that the subcontractors are Hagenauer's responsibility. It could be proactively formulated ("Is it true that Brisen is not paying the subcontractors?") and commented on it.

Given the nature of the contract with Hagenauer as a general contractor, it was Hagenauer's responsibility to engage the subcontractors, hire them and then pay them. There is no contract between Brisen and the subcontractors. Depending on the progress of the construction work, the instalments paid by Brisen to Hagenauer also included the subcontractors' costs, which were to be paid by Hagenauer to the subcontractor. Currently we cannot rule out the possibility that not all payments made by Brisen to Hagenauer to settle the subcontractors' fees were actually passed on to the subcontractors. Enquiries are currently underway regarding this matter, on which no further comment will be made.

raw_content:

Date: 2026-04-01

Messages: 1

--- Christophe Buchwalder (2026-04-01 17:10) ---
Dear Dimitry, dear Edita,

I hope you are doing well.

Ramon did not revert to me on the interest payment after our last, blunt reply.

Please find attached a new letter in French that Ramon Rodriguez sent us together with a (bad) automated translation into English.

In the letter, Ramon Rodriguez uses the publicity around the situation of Hagenauer Austria to raise questions about LCG’s solvency.

If correct, I would suggest to reply as follows:

“Dear Colleague,

I acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 31 March 2026.

I accept your apologies for sending me the letter in registered form and take the liberty to reply to it on behalf of LCG by this simple email.

Hagenauer Austria GmbH & Co KG acted as total contractor for the construction of the hotel in Vienna and delivered the project over five months late and with over 2’000 defects.

This has led to a suspension of the payments to Hagenauer Austria GmbH & Co KG pending a remediation of the situation.

As is apparent from the public information on the matter, the situation does not affect LCG’s solvency or that of any of its affiliates but only that of Hagenauer Austria GmbH & Co KG. Neither does the situation impact the operation of the hotel or RG7’s revenues.

This email is sent to you for the sake of transparency only and, for the avoidance of doubt, without acknowledgement of any disclosure obligation for that matter under the information undertakings of the loan agreement.

Yours sincerely,”

Please take the opportunity of Ramon Rodriguez’ email to ensure that LCG is in compliance with the standard information undertakings of Section 12.

For the avoidance of doubt, the deadline to April 7th that Ramon Rodriguez is imparting in his letter for our reply has no legal or contractual basis.

If you are around over Easter and wish to spend some time together, let me know. It would be nice to catch up.

With kind regards,

Christophe Buchwalder
